title: Why Engineering and Architecture Practices Finish June Busy and Start August Tight on Cash
excerpt: Engineering practices often record their strongest June billings, driven by government budget deadlines. But WIP timing and 30-day payment terms mean the August cash position looks quite different from what June implies.
content:
Engineering and architecture consultancies across Queensland and NSW are in their busiest billing period of the year. Government clients, councils, and infrastructure agencies working against June 30 capital budget deadlines have been issuing project instructions at a pace that rarely appears in any other month. The fee register fills. For most consultancies, June will produce the strongest revenue figure of the year.

The August bank account often tells a different story.

This pattern repeats with enough consistency that it should be a planned-for item rather than a source of surprise. Understanding it precisely, including the mechanics that create the gap between June activity and August cash, is one of the more useful financial exercises available in the week before the financial year closes.

How the WIP Register Builds Through June

For engineering and architecture practices working on time-and-materials commissions, every hour a fee earner works accumulates in the work in progress register until the invoice goes out. When June project activity runs at its highest pace, WIP accumulates fastest.

The billing lag depends on how the practice invoices. On time-and-materials commissions, most firms invoice monthly in arrears: June hours are captured in the timesheet cycle that closes at month end, and the invoice goes out in the first week of July. On milestone-payment projects, billing occurs when a design stage is signed off. For work completed at pace in late June, that sign-off may not happen until an approval step that falls after the financial year closes.

The result is that a practice with strong June activity may carry a significant WIP balance at June 30 and wait until August for the cash to arrive, even though the June revenue figure looks healthy. Government clients typically operate on 30-day payment terms. An invoice sent in early July for June hours settles in mid-August. For a consultancy with a material share of annual billing concentrated in the June push, the gap between work completed and cash received can represent several weeks of operating costs sitting in the collection cycle.

July's obligations do not pause while that cash travels. The first payroll of the new financial year runs at new award rates from the first full pay period after 1 July. The Q4 superannuation guarantee for the April to June quarter falls due on 28 July, representing three months of super accrual as a single outflow. Both land in the same four-week window as the bulk of June invoices still clearing the collection cycle.

A 13-week cash flow model built before June 30, updated with the July WIP billing schedule, the new payroll costs, and the Q4 super liability, maps this week-by-week. For most consultancies that have not run this exercise, the July picture is tighter than the June 30 bank balance suggests.

What the Billable Ratio Shows at Year End

Billable ratio is the share of each fee earner's working time charged to client projects. A practice where fee earners collectively work ten hours and bill seven to clients has a 70 percent billable ratio for that period. Across the full practice, this metric determines how efficiently the most significant cost, professional time, converts to fee revenue.

For most engineering and architecture consultancies, a practice-wide billable ratio between 65 and 75 percent is strong. Below 60 percent, the overhead absorbed by non-billable time, including business development, internal meetings, and project management tasks not recovered in fees, begins to suppress profitability materially. Every percentage point of improvement across the fee-earning team adds revenue without adding direct cost.

With twelve months of timesheet data available at EOFY, the full-year billable ratio by fee earner and by project type becomes visible for the first time. The comparisons most practices find revealing are not the overall average but the spread: which fee earners consistently track above the practice average, which project types produce the strongest utilisation, and whether the government work driving the June billing surge produces genuinely strong utilisation across the year or concentrated activity in a narrow window that masks a lower average through the other months.

A Workforce Capacity and Utilisation Review maps this picture across the team using the year's timesheet data, identifying where capacity is being deployed efficiently and where adjustments to project roles or fee recovery structures would improve the ratio without adding headcount.

The Longer-Term Picture at EOFY

For practice principals thinking about medium-term direction, whether that involves structured growth, bringing in a second principal, or an eventual transition, the EOFY accounts provide the most complete picture available of where the business stands against the factors that shape its valuation.

Engineering and architecture consultancies typically attract three to five times normalised profit when they change hands. The spread within that range is driven by how much of the practice's revenue and key client relationships depend on the principal personally, how stable billable utilisation is across the year, how concentrated the fee book is across a small number of clients, and whether the practice is genuinely transferable to new leadership. Businesses at the top of that range have typically worked deliberately on all of these factors over a period of years before any transition process begins.

An Exit Readiness Diagnostic at this point in the year, when twelve months of complete project and financial data are available, identifies which structural factors are most compressing the multiple and what specific changes would move it in the years before a planned transition. For most consultancy principals, the findings centre on principal dependence in key client relationships and the degree of practice independence as the two factors with the clearest path to improvement.

ProfitPulse works with engineering, architecture, and technical consultancy practices across Queensland and the East Coast at exactly this point in the financial calendar. If June has produced the year's strongest billing and the July cash position has not yet been mapped against the Q4 super obligation and new payroll costs, that picture is worth building before the year closes. Book a discovery call with ProfitPulse.

faqs.q: What is a good billable ratio for an engineering consultancy in Australia?
faqs.a: Billable ratio is the share of each fee earner's total working time charged to client projects. For most engineering and architecture consultancies, a practice-wide ratio between 65 and 75 percent is considered strong. Below 60 percent, non-billable overhead absorbs enough professional time to suppress profitability materially. The most revealing analysis is not the practice average but the spread: comparing ratios by individual fee earner and project type shows where capacity is being deployed less efficiently and where targeted adjustment would improve the result.

faqs.q: Why does my engineering or architecture firm feel cash-tight in July in Australia?
faqs.a: Three cash demands concentrate in July that are individually manageable but collectively significant. The first payroll of the new financial year runs at new award rates from 1 July. The Q4 superannuation guarantee for the April to June quarter falls due on 28 July. And the bulk of June invoices, issued in early July for work completed in the final weeks of June, typically settle in mid-August on 30-day government client terms. The gap between what June produced and when that cash arrives determines how comfortable July actually feels.

faqs.q: What is WIP in a professional services firm and how does it affect cash flow?
faqs.a: Work in progress in a professional services firm is the value of fee earner time completed but not yet invoiced. When a practice bills monthly in arrears, June hours appear in the WIP register until the invoice goes out in early July. At EOFY, the WIP balance represents work already earned but not yet converted to cash. Lenders and buyers examine WIP quality when assessing a consultancy, looking at how current it is and whether it consistently converts to cash at the stated rate without write-downs.

faqs.q: What EBITDA multiple does an engineering or architecture consultancy attract in Australia?
faqs.a: Engineering and architecture consultancies in Australia typically attract three to five times normalised annual profit. Businesses at the lower end carry significant principal dependence, concentrated client relationships, and variable billable ratios. Those at the upper end have a capable team that operates with reasonable independence from the founder, stable utilisation across the year, and a fee book that would logically transfer with the business. An indicative business valuation establishes where a specific practice sits within that range and which structural factors are most shaping the multiple.

faqs.q: How do I improve billable utilisation in my engineering or architecture practice?
faqs.a: The most direct levers are reviewing how administration, business development, and project management tasks are allocated across the team, whether fee earners are absorbing non-billable work that does not need to come to them, and whether any project types consistently generate lower billable hours than the fee structure implies. A Workforce Capacity and Utilisation Review maps the current position by fee earner and project type, identifying where the ratio is most improvable and what structural adjustments would produce the clearest gain without disrupting current project delivery.

faqs.q: What financial metrics should I track in my engineering consultancy each month?
faqs.a: Five metrics most directly signal financial health in an engineering or architecture practice: billable ratio across the fee-earning team, WIP days measured from work completion to invoice, debtor days from invoice to cash receipt, project margin by commission type, and client concentration measured as the top three clients' share of total fee revenue. Together these tell you whether the practice is converting professional capacity into cash efficiently and where the highest-value improvements sit. Most practices already hold the data to calculate all five.

faqs.q: How does a fractional CFO help an engineering or architecture practice in Queensland?
faqs.a: A fractional CFO arrangement for an engineering or architecture practice typically installs the WIP tracking, billable ratio monitoring, and forward cash modelling that allow a principal to manage the business commercially rather than reactively. For practices between two and fifteen million in fee revenue, the monthly financial review covers WIP ageing, utilisation by fee earner, the July-August cash transition, and any lender or valuation conversations the business is navigating. Most principals find the financial clarity this creates pays back within the first two to three months of the engagement.
